coredata.dc:title: Fluorescent carbon dots as selective nano probe for determination of diacerein in presence of co-formulated drugs
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Rizk, Mohamed
Toubar, Safaa
Ramzy, Emad
Helmy, Marwa I.
coredata.dc:description: In this work, a new and simple carbon dots (CDs) based fluorescent probe was introduced for selective determination of diacerein (DIA) in presence of two co-formulated drugs. This highly fluorescent sensor was constructed using chitosan as a carbon and nitrogen source by single step carbonization. The constructed probe is based on the inner filter effect (IFE), in which DIA serves as a strong absorber, influencing the excitation of the fluorescer (CDs). This overlap leads to quenching of CDs fluorescence upon increasing DIA concentration within the range of 2.5–17.5 µg/mL with mean % recovery reached to 99.7 ± 0.7. The performance of the constructed sensor had been validated according to the ICH guidelines and the results revealed that it is precise and accurate. Moreover, it has many advantages such as simplicity, saving time and good selectivity for the determination of DIA as a minor component in presence of co-formulated drugs in its tablet dosage form.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Chitosan- Carbon dots
Diacerein
Inner filter effect
Fluorescent nano- probe

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: General procedure for the determination of DIA
Determination of DIA in presence of GLU, MSM, and chondroitin sulfate
Analytical application for determination of pharmaceutical samples
Synthesis and characterization of chitosan derived CDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images of the prepared CDs.
FT-IR spectrum of the prepared CDs.

[A] Absorption spectra of CDs and DIA before mixing and the absorption spectrum of DIA after mixing with CDs using CDs solution as a blank. [B] Stern–Volmer curves for quenching the fluorescence of CDs by DIA at two different temperatures. [C] Emission spectra of the prepared CDs at different excitation wavelengths. [D,E] effect of different pHs on the emission spectrum of the prepared CDs.

[A] Incubation time effect on the FI of CDs [B] Fluorescence emission spectra of CDs in the presence of various concentrations of DIA. [C] Calibaration curve of DIA. [D] effect of co formulated drugs at different concentrations on the FI of CDs.
Performance data for DIA using the constructed fluorescent sensor.
[A] Determination of DIA in bulk alone and in laboratory prepared mixtures with co formulated drugs. [B] Determination of DIA in laboratory prepared tablet dosage form.

row.entry: DIA laboratory prepared mix with GLU and MSM
DIA conc (μg/mL) in laboratory prepared tablet dosage form with GLU and MSM

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: precision data for DIA determinations using the proposed sensing system.
statistical comparison of the results obtained from the analysis of the laboratory prepared tablet mixture utilizing the proposed method and the reported method [12].

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: b
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Tabulated t and F values at p = 0.05.

head.ce:title: Fluorescent carbon dots as selective nano probe for determination of diacerein in presence of co-formulated drugs

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Analytical Chemistry Department, Faculty of Pharmacy, Helwan University, Ein Helwan, 11795 Cairo, Egypt
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Analytical Chemistry Department, Faculty of Pharmacy, Helwan University, Ein Helwan, 11795 Cairo, Egypt

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, a new and simple carbon dots (CDs) based fluorescent probe was introduced for selective determination of diacerein (DIA) in presence of two co-formulated drugs. This highly fluorescent sensor was constructed using chitosan as a carbon and nitrogen source by single step carbonization. The constructed probe is based on the inner filter effect (IFE), in which DIA serves as a strong absorber, influencing the excitation of the fluorescer (CDs). This overlap leads to quenching of CDs fluorescence upon increasing DIA concentration within the range of 2.5–17.5 µg/mL with mean % recovery reached to 99.7 ± 0.7. The performance of the constructed sensor had been validated according to the ICH guidelines and the results revealed that it is precise and accurate. Moreover, it has many advantages such as simplicity, saving time and good selectivity for the determination of DIA as a minor component in presence of co-formulated drugs in its tablet dosage form.

ce:section.ce:para: Upon surveying the literature it was found that several methods were established for analysis of DIA alone or combined with other drugs such as spectrophotometric methods [2–5], chromatographic methods [6–12] and electrochemical methods [13]. HPLC method was reported for simultaneous determination of DIA and the previously two mentioned drugs [12] which was used as a reported method in our study. To the best of our knowledge there is no spectrofluorimetric method for the determination of DIA.

So the aim of this research is the development of fast, simple and selective fluorescent probe for determination of DIA in bulk and pharmaceutical preparations. Moreover, this work aims at determining DIA as a minor component in presence of GLU and MSM as co formulated drugs.

Fluorescent probes are increasingly employed in monitoring and sensing applications owing to their selectivity, cheap cost, and simplicity. CDs are carbon nanomaterials with unique optical and electrical characteristics that have particle sizes ranging from 2 to 10 nm [14]. CDs are more favorable than conventional metal quantum dots and organic dyes because of their excellent water solubility, photo bleaching stability, improved biocompatibility, and low toxicity and these properties enhanced its use in the field of analytical chemistry. The interaction of analytes and CDs is the basis for these applications which lead to either quenching or recovery of their fluorescence [15].

Static and dynamic quenching, energy transfer, photo induced electron transfer, and the inner filter effect (IFE) might all be used to quench the fluorescence of CDs. Unlike other fluorescence sensing methods that rely on intermolecular interactions between the sensor and analyte, which need being at a certain distance or having a complimentary geometry, the IFE does not require being at a specific distance or having a complementary geometry [15]. IFE-based sensors are more versatile, time-saving, and straightforward as the CDs-analyte connection was not required as the IFE is caused by the analyte's absorption of excitation and/or emission light during the sensing process. With IFE-based probes, higher sensitivity and a lower detection limit may be achieved when compared to analyte absorbance alone, since changes in analyte absorbance translate into exponential changes in CDs fluorescence [16]. In the present research, CDs were prepared from chitosan via very simple approach and as a consequence amino-functionalized CDs was produced via one-step carbonization handling of chitosan as [17] with some modifications. The as prepared CDs were then utilized as selective fluorescent probe for selective determination of DIA in bulk and in presence of GLU and MSM as co -formulated drugs. This probe is constructed based on the IFE mechanism and to the best of our knowledge this is the first CDs-based fluorescent probe for the determination of DIA. So this work aims at elucidating a rapid, simple, and selective fluorescent CDs based probe for the determination of DIA in its combined dosage form in presence of GLU and MSM, and using this simple technique instead of the tedious, time consuming, and expensive HPLC technique [12] for the routine analysis in the quality control laboratories.
DIA of purity 99.6%, chondroitin sulfate, GLU and MSM were obtained from (EVA PHARMA, Cairo, Egypt) as gratis samples.

Diacerein capsule (Batch No. 2102121) labeled to contain 50 mg of DIA with expiration date 2/2023 and Genuphil tablet (Batch No. 2011721) labeled to contain 375 mg of GLU, 375 mg MSM and 300 mg chondroitin sulfate with expiration date 11/2023 (EVA PHARMA, Cairo, Egypt) were obtained from the local market.

The acid combination containing 0.04 mol/L phosphoric acid (Sigma-Aldrich), 0.04 mol/L acetic acid (Loba Chemie Co, India), and 0.04 mol/L in boric acid(adwic, Cairo, Egypt) was used to prepare Britton-Robinson (BR) buffer. To get the required pH 2.44–9.1, the pH of the buffer solutions were adjusted with the appropriate quantity of 0.2 mol/L sodium hydroxide (Sigma-Aldrich).
Chitosan was obtained from (Sigma Aldrich). Methanol HPLC grade (Thermo Fisher Scientific Chemicals, USA).

UV – visible absorption spectra of the prepared CDs was obtained using double beam JASCO (V-530) UV – Visible Spectrophotometer using 2 matched 1.0 cm quartz cuvettes. The fluorescence spectra and measurements were obtained on JASCO FP-6200 – Spectrofluorometer equipped with a 150 W xenon lamp and using 1.0 cm quartz cell, instrument excitation and emission slits were both adjusted to 10 nm. The particle size of the prepared CDs was determined by JEOL JEM-1400 transmission electron microscope (TEM) with accelerating voltage equals to 80 KV. Fourier transform-infrared (FTIR) spectrum of CDs was drawn at room temperature using Shimadzu FTIR spectrophotometer with serial number (A224156).

The CDs were made in the same way as [17], but with a certain modifications. Chitosan (0.5 g) was placed in a crucible and heated at 270 °C on a hot plate in an air environment for 30 min, yielding a dark product. After allowing the solution to cool to ambient temperature, 50 mL of methanol was added then the solution was stirred using the magnetic stirrer at 900 rpm at 25 °C for 10 min and the mixture was sonicated for 5 min then the solution was filtered using 0.22 μm syringe filter and the resulted clear solution was kept at refrigerator at 4 °C for further characterization.

ce:section.ce:section-title: General procedure for the determination of DIA
ce:section.ce:para: DIA (100 μg/mL) standard stock solution was prepared by dissolving 10.0 mg of DIA standard at 50 mL methanol using 100 mL volumetric flask then the solution was completed to the mark with the same solvent. The flask was immersed in hot water bath for 2 min. To a series of 10 mL stoppered volumetric flasks, 500 μL of CDs stock solution was accurately transferred then various aliquots from the DIA standard stock solution were added to give final concentrations within the range of 2.5 μg/mL to 17.5 μg/mL of DIA. After excitation at 325 nm, the fluorescence intensities were measured at 412 nm. Plotting the fluorescence intensity against the corresponding drug concentration (μg/mL) yielded the calibration curve.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of DIA in presence of GLU, MSM, and chondroitin sulfate
ce:section.ce:para: GLU (1 mg/mL) stock solution was prepared by transferring 10.0 mg of GLU standard to 10 mL volumetric flask contains 5 mL deionized water, dissolved well and completed to the mark with methanol. The same procedures were followed for preparation of the standard stock solution (1 mg/mL) of chondroitin sulfate. MSM (1 mg/mL) stock solution was prepared by dissolving 10.0 mg of MSM standard in 10 mL methanol. To examine the potential interference from each co–formulated drug, different concentrations were prepared as proceeded under [general procedure for the determination of DIA section] to give final concentrations of 75 μg/mL, 187.5 μg/mL, and 225 μg/mL for GLU and 25, 62.5, and 75 μg/mL regarding MSM and chondroitin sulfate. After that different laboratory prepared mixtures were constructed for determining DIA in presence of GLU and MSM as co-formulated drugs as following; to a series of 10 mL volumetric flasks different aliquots from the stock solutions of DIA, GLU and MSM were transferred to give 5 μg/mL: 75 μg/mL: 25 μg/mL, 12.5 μg/mL: 187.5 μg/mL: 62.5 μg/mL and 15 μg/mL: 225 μg/mL: 75 μg/mL for DIA, GLU and MSM, respectively. The % recoveries of DIA were calculated from the regression equation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical application for determination of pharmaceutical samples
ce:section.ce:para: DIA is co-formulated with GLU and MSM in tablet dosage form, this dosage form contains 50 mg DIA, 750 mg GLU, and 250 mg MSM but this dosage form does not available in the local Egyptian market, so a laboratory prepared mix that contains 10 mg, 150 mg, and 150 mg from DIA, GLU,and MSM, respectively was prepared. The continent of ten diacerein 50 mg capsules were separately weighed and mixed well, and then the amount of the powder equivalent to 10 mg DIA was weighted. Regarding GLU and MSM, ten genuphil tablets each contains 325 mg of GLU and MSM were separately weighed then they were grinded well and the amount of the powder equivalent to 150 mg GLU and MSM was weighed and mixed well with DIA powder in suitable container then the powder mix was accurately transferred to 100 mL volumetric flask containing 50 mL methanol and sonicated for 15 min at 25° C then completed to the mark with methanol and immersed in hot water bath for 2 min. The solution was filtered using 0.45 μm syringe filter then different aliquots from the resulted clear solution were withdrawn to give final concentrations of 5 μg/mL, 10 μg/mL and 15 μg/mL of DIA and the mean % recoveries of DIA were calculated from the corresponding regression equation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of chitosan derived CDs
ce:section.ce:para: As mentioned previously, the amino-functionalized CDs were made by carbonizing chitosan in a single process. Methanol was used as a solvent because the high solubility of CDs in methanol and the produced fluorescence emission spectrum has better shape and higher intensity. Under daylight, the produced CDs' solution was yellowish, translucent, and clear, with a strong blue luminescence under UV excitation. TEM was used to study the particles size of the produced CDs. The CDs were homogeneous, mono dispersed, and well separated from one another with particle diameters in the range of 4.20–10.4 nm, as shown in Fig. 2 .The function groups on the surface of CDs were investigated using FT-IR spectroscopy, peak at 3350 cm−1 corresponding to OH/NH, peaks at 2950 cm−1 and 2850 cm−1 corresponding to stretching vibration of CH aliphatic, peak at 1475 cm−1 corresponding to aromatic CC stretching/NH bending and peak at 1350 cm−1 assigned to stretching vibration of CN as shown in Fig. 3 .

To investigate the optical characteristics of the prepared CDs, the UV–Vis and fluorescence spectra were recorded. Two kinds of fluorescence emission processes have been proposed, despite the fact that the fluorescence emission mechanisms are still disputed. The first class mechanism is generated by the band gap transitions due to conjugated ח - *חdomains, whereas the second class mechanism is caused by the presence of numerous surface defects [18]. The UV–Vis absorption and fluorescence spectra of as-prepared CDs were examined. The UV–Vis absorption spectrum showed that a significant UV–Vis absorption peak at 262 nm as illustrated in Fig. 4 A, which was generally ascribed to the ח - *חtransition of aromatic sp2 domains from the carbon core [19,20]. When stimulated at the maximal excitation wavelength of 325 nm, the as-prepared CDs showed a significant emission peak at 412 nm, as seen in Fig. 4C. Under visible light, the solution of as-prepared CDs exhibits clear and transparent yellow color, but under UV light (365 nm) illumination, it fluoresced brightly blue. The quantum yield (QY) was calculated according to the reported method in [21] after excitation at 325 nm and it was found to be 44.38%. Above the pH 8.1, the photoluminescence intensity of the CDs significantly reduced, but not significantly changed when the pH was lower than 8.1 and at pH 8.1 it slightly increased. The emission peak did not shift while the pH value was changed, indicating that it is pH independent as shown in Fig. 4 (D, E). Furthermore, in Fig. 4C, the phenomena of excitation dependence were clearly seen. The emission spectra were excitation wavelength-dependent, upon increasing the excitation wavelength from 310 to 390 nm. The emission peak showed stronger FI after excitation at 325 nm and after that the emission spectra show red shift with remarkable decrease in the FI. All the measurements were carried out at 412 nm after excitation at 325 nm.

It was found that DIA has an effective quenching effect on the produced CDs; allowing its determination to be successful using this CDs. Fig. 4A Illustrates that DIA's UV spectrum has great overlap with the excitation spectrum of CDs, implying that fluorescence quenching is linked to IFE [16]. The fluorescence quenching efficiency (F0/F) was calculated using the Stern-Volmer equation: F0/F = KSV[Q] + 1 where F0 and F are the FIs of the CDs in the absence and presence of DIA, respectively, KSV is the Stern-Volmer quenching constant and [Q] is DIA solution concentration. Quenching of CDs fluorescence could be caused by different mechanisms such as static quenching, dynamic quenching, and IFE [15]. In static quenching, the Stern-Volmer quenching constant decreases upon increasing the temperature but in case of dynamic quenching, it increases with increasing temperature. The KSV is unaffected by temperature in the case of IFE [16]. Thus, the quenching efficiency was investigated at two distinct temperatures to explore the presence of complementary quenching processes with IFE Fig. 4B. At 277° K and 328° K, the KSV was determined to be 1.4129 × 104 M−1and 1.4045 × 104 M−1, respectively, ignoring the static and dynamic quenching processes. IFE is not considered as quenching mechanism but it occurs due to masking the excitation light or filtering off the fluorescer emission, so the absorption spectra of the prepared CDs or DIA should not be varied [16]. As illustrated in Fig. 4A the absorption spectrum of DIA does not change in presence of CDs using the same concentration of the prepared CDs as a blank. The same thing was occurred to the absorption spectrum of the prepared CDs in case of considering the DIA solution with same concentration as a blank.

After adding DIA to the prepared CDs, the impact of incubation duration on the fluorescence quenching of CDs was investigated. From 1 to 15 min, the fluorescence intensity was recorded at 1-minute intervals. The fluorescence of CDs was quenched quickly after adding DIA, taking only 1 min. The sensor's response remained constant for 15 min as shown in Fig. 5 A.

The validation of the developed fluorescent probe was achieved according to the ICH guidelines [22]. After optimizing the sensing conditions, the calibration curve of DIA was constructed by increasing its concentration and drawing it against the produced FI of the prepared CDs, the resulted calibration curve showed good linearity over the concentration range of (2.5–17.5 µg/mL) of DIA as shown in Fig. 5 B,C and Table1 .The limit of quantitation (LOQ) and limit of detection(LOD) were calculated as stated by ICH guidelines from the following equations, 10 σ/S and 3.3 σ/S where σ and S are the standard deviation of intercept and the slope of the calibration curve, respectively. The accuracy of the developed sensing system was confirmed over the established concentration range in presence of the co-formulated drugs in bulk and laboratory prepared mixture from the resulted acceptable % recoveries as illustrated in Table 2 .The precision of the developed sensing system was proved from The minor values of standard deviation (SD) of intra-day and inter-day values illustrated in Table 3 , demonstrating the good precision of the developed sensing system. The selectivity of the developed sensing system was proved by determination of DIA in presence of the co formulated drugs Table 2, the results showed that there was no interference occurred from these drugs during analysis of DIA as a minor component in bulk or in its combined tablet dosage form as shown Table 2 and Fig. 5D. In comparison of the reported HPLC method [12] for analysis of DIA and the two co formulated drugs the developed sensor is rapid, simple and more economic as the reported method consumed large quantities of expensive solvents and take longer time.

The developed sensing system for DIA based on the quenching of the FI of the chitosan derived CDs is rapid, simple and economic and was able to determine DIA as a minor component in presence of the co formulated drugs. The prepared CDs were derived using one step carbonization from a cheap and readily accessible single starting ingredient (chitosan), resulting in a highly fluorescent CDs based sensor with narrow particle size distribution 4.20–10.4 nm. Based on the IFE mechanism, DIA effectively quenched the fluorescence of the produced probe leading to a quick response and long-stability fluorescence signals. DIA has been quantified over a wide concentration range with a LOQ of 2.2 µg/mL and a LOD of 0.7 µg/mL. The developed sensing system is accurate and precise allowing the selective determination of DIA in its tablet dosage form in presence of the co formulated drugs, furthermore it can be applied for routine analysis of DIA in the quality control laboratories.

Mohamed Rizk: Conceptualization, Methodology, Supervision, Resources, Writing – review & editing. Safaa Toubar: Conceptualization, Methodology, Visualization, Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Emad Ramzy: Software, Data curation, Formal analysis, Validation, Writing – review & editing. Marwa I. Helmy: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:para: The authors acknowledge the financial support from NERHADOU INTERNATIONAL.
For pharmaceuticals and Nutraceuticals company, Cairo, Egypt.
The auther(s)received no financial support for the research, authorship, and/or publication of this article.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots: facile synthesis and application as a “turn-off” fluorescent probe for detection of Hg2+ ions
Biosensors Bioelectron.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: R.Zhang and W. Chen , Nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots: facile synthesis and application as a “turn-off” fluorescent probe for detection of Hg2+ ions, Biosensors and Bioelectronics. 55(2014) 83-90.

ce:other-ref.ce:textref: ICH harmonised tripartite guidelines (2005) validation of analytical procedures: Text and methodology Q2(R1).
